title: Kafta bil sanieh
subtitle: Tray-baked spiced lamb mince in tomato sauce
excerpt: Lebanese tray bake: spiced lamb mince mixed with onion and parsley, formed into oval patties, layered with sliced tomatoes and potatoes, then baked in a rich tomato sauce until the potatoes are tender and the kafta is cooked through. A one-tray dish that improves with 10 minutes of resting after it comes out of the oven.
sourceNotes: Kafta bil sanieh (kafta in a tray) is a staple of Lebanese home cooking, documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) and Claudia Roden's 'A Book of Middle Eastern Food' (1968). The dish is a common family meal across Lebanon, Syria, and Palestine, often served directly from the baking dish at the table.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked and cooled dish for up to 3 months. Reheat covered at 180°C fan for 25 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble the dish up to a day ahead and refrigerate before baking. Add 5 to 10 minutes to the baking time if cooking from cold.

content.text: Kafta is the Levantine word for spiced minced meat, shaped into patties, rolls, or meatballs. In this tray version, the formed kafta are laid on a bed of potatoes, covered with sliced tomatoes and a diluted tomato sauce, then baked until everything is cooked through and the sauce has reduced around the edges. The potatoes absorb the lamb fat and the tomato liquid and end up somewhere between roasted and braised.

The kafta mixture should be well seasoned — allspice and cinnamon are the defining flavours of Lebanese minced meat, alongside cumin and a generous amount of flat-leaf parsley. Mix the meat by hand, squeezing it together until the seasonings are evenly distributed and the mixture feels cohesive. Chilling for 20 minutes before forming makes the patties easier to shape.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Combine the {{lamb-mince}}, grated {{onion}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{allspice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} in a bowl. Mix by hand until well combined. Form into 12 oval patties about 2 cm thick.

Drizzle the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} over the base of the baking tin. Arrange the sliced {{potato}} in a single layer across the bottom. Lay the kafta patties on top of the potatoes. Arrange the sliced {{tomato}} over the kafta. Blend the {{tinned-tomatoes}} smooth and pour over everything. Season with salt. Cover tightly with foil and bake for 30 minutes. Remove the foil and bake for a further 15 minutes until the sauce has reduced and the top is slightly coloured. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Potatoes are still firm after baking
items.cause: Sliced too thick or oven temperature too low
items.fix: Slice the potatoes no thicker than 5 mm so they cook through in the allotted time. If still firm after 45 minutes, cover and return for another 15 minutes

items.cause: Too much foil time removed or mince too lean
items.fix: Lamb mince with a reasonable fat content stays moist in the sauce. Keep the foil on for the first 30 minutes to trap steam

items.cause: Tinned tomatoes not well blended or foil removed too early
items.fix: Remove the foil only for the last 15 minutes so the sauce can reduce. The sauce should be thickened and coating everything when done

items.cause: Mixture not well combined or resting time skipped
items.fix: Squeeze the mixture firmly when mixing and when forming. Rest for 10 minutes after baking — the juices redistribute and the kafta firms up slightly

content.text: Kafta bil sanieh is Lebanese family cooking at its most practical: a single tray that feeds four, requires no watching, and comes to the table in the dish it was cooked in. It belongs to the broad tradition of Levantine tray bakes, a family of dishes in which protein and vegetables are layered in a deep tin and cooked in a tomato-based sauce. The kafta seasoning is recognisably Lebanese — allspice and cinnamon are the characteristic spices of the region's minced meat dishes, distinguishing them from Turkish or Iranian equivalents.
